title: Cirrus: rootless system tests: local, not remote
body:
The new CI setup (accidentally?) ran rootless system tests
with podman-remote instead of local, and only on fedora.
This PR runs rootless system tests using podman local,
and on all four flavors (fedora + ubuntu).

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

@cevich I'm stumped - ubuntu tests are [failing because environment variables are lost over ssh](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/7957/checks?check_run_id=1222587361). That doesn't confuse me - what confuses me is, how can this possibly work on Fedora?

[Found it](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4708224811139072?command=setup#L183-L186):
```bash
    # If not running interactively, don't do anything
    case $- in
        *i*) ;;
          *) return;;
    esac
```
Well, that's annoying. Fixing it is going to require the VM dance, so it'll have to wait until next week.

@cevich PTAL (low priority). CI is still running but it seems to have passed "sys podman ubuntu-* rootless host", the tests in question. Even so, there's always something I've missed when dealing with CI...

> Even so, there's always something I've missed when dealing with CI...

Have no fear, I've committed an entire hemisphere of my brain to remembering all the moving parts :grinning: 

I believe the other missing piece here is `/etc/environment`.  We drop all our important env. vars into there during `setup_environment.sh` and then hope and pray it gets loaded just in the nick of time.  It's easily possible I forgot something.  

The non-interactive thing you found is also real problem.  TBH I very much don't care for the way Ubuntu does shell setup.

In any case, loading `lib.sh` guarantees `/etc/environment` is loaded, so as long as the needed env. vars are set in there, it should be all well and good once `runner.sh` kicks in.
Oh there's code here...lemmie peek at that too...

Hmmmm, trying to think of simplifying the (already complex) situation...What about dropping the loading function, and instead just have `lib.sh` always, conditionally load the user env. file?

We know that whenever we see `$UID != 0` and `$PRIV_NAME == rootless`, we're very likely executing `runner.sh` intentionally as a user for some rootless testing.  I cannot think of any current or possible future setup where that won't be the case.

@edsantiago did you happen to take a peek at what actually ends up in the user's `$envfile`?  Assuming a VM is never used for more than one kind of test (it isn't) or style of test (it isn't) I'm wondering why everything can't simply exist in /etc/environment?  Otherwise I'm okay with your solution, as long as a simpler one is not possible within a reasonable amount of time.  Taking a quick poke at this with `get_ci_vm.sh`...

>did you happen to take a peek at what actually ends up in the user's $envfile?

Only indirectly, by looking at the [debug output from setup_rootless()](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5919914307354624?command=setup#L174). (Also indirectly, from the fact that CI passed, including Ubuntu rootless).

I considered using `/etc/environment` but quickly rejected that because there's already a lot of code in `setup_environment.sh` that appends to that file, but nothing that actually resets it. The script code also includes 'export', which according to my reasing of `environment(5)` is invalid. I just didn't want to go down that rabbit hole.

...here's what I got:
```
[root@cevich-fedora-c4948709391728640 podman]# cat /home/some4292dude/cirrus-task-environment
# Added by contrib/cirrus/lib.sh setup_rootless()
export SETUP_ENVIRONMENT=1
export CGROUP_MANAGER=systemd
export CI=false
export CIRRUS_BASE_SHA=YOU_FOUND_A_BUG
export CIRRUS_BUILD_ID=861602520879
export CIRRUS_CI=false
export CIRRUS_REPO_NAME=podman
export CIRRUS_WORKING_DIR=/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman
export DISTRO_NV=fedora-32
export GOCACHE=/var/tmp/go/cache/go-build
export GOPATH=/var/tmp/go
export GOSRC=/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman
export OCI_RUNTIME=/usr/bin/crun
export PODBIN_NAME=podman
export PRIV_NAME=rootless
export ROOTLESS_USER=some4292dude
export SCRIPT_BASE=./contrib/cirrus
export TEST_ENVIRON=host
export TEST_FLAVOR=sys

[root@cevich-fedora-c4948709391728640 podman]# cat /etc/environment
export TEST_FLAVOR=sys
export PRIV_NAME=rootless
export PODBIN_NAME=podman
export TEST_ENVIRON=host
export DISTRO_NV=fedora-32
export OCI_RUNTIME=/usr/bin/crun
export CGROUP_MANAGER=systemd
export ROOTLESS_USER='some4292dude'
```

Notably missing are all of the other default `$CIRRUS_*` variables.  Even still, I can't think of a reason why having those set system-wide (`/etc/environment`) would cause problems.  The values never change within the scope/lifetime of the VM.  I also don't see any variables/values that need to be different on the VM when executing as root vs rootless.  The important one that must never get hard-coded is `$CONTAINER` but IIRC, we already filter for that.

So given the very limited and specific scope for a VM now (running one test in a single format) I think it's okay to just stick everything in `/etc/environment`.  I dunno about 'export' vs non in that file, so I assumed the worse-case (sub-process needs the var and doesn't get it).

...Oh in fact, there's a bug.  `export SETUP_ENVIRONMENT=1` is suppose to be the 'canary' for `setup_environment.sh`.  It should be set in `/etc/environment` and isn't.  That's really only a minor thing though, protecting users of `get_ci_vm.sh` from doing a double-setup (will never happen under normal CI conditions).

@cevich what I'm trying to say is that I believe the use of `/etc/environment` in these scripts is broken (the format is clearly documented as "VAR=VALUE"). I just don't feel like dealing with it right now because I just know that it will be another all-day or many-day expedition. My goal here was simply to get rootless tests working on Ubuntu.

I'm okay with not fixing the use of `export` in `/etc/environment`, for now, that does seem likely to break things.  I also don't want a many-day expedition.  Are you willing to try re-using `/etc/environment`? If it doesn't work, then I'm okay with the more complex solution.

I've just spent an hour trying to understand `/etc/environment`, and I give up. It's not going to get me where I need to go.

For posterity, my findings:
* variables defined in the file _are_ global (i.e. no `export` is needed); and
* `export` seems to be ignored (eg `export FOO=bar` sets `$FOO` in a login shell); and
* the file is _not consulted_ on `ssh` through a multiplexed session, but as expected this can be overridden via `ssh -o 'ControlMaster no' -o 'ControlPath /dev/null'`. (I do not expect that this would be a problem with the CI use of ssh); and
* I tested only on f32, and have no idea how this would work on Ubuntu.

I do believe that the current use of `/etc/environment` in `contrib/cirrus/setup_environment.sh` is improper and risky. It is owned by and intended for `pam`, it should not have `export` directives, and it should not be `sourced` by client code. But our CI setup is too fragile for me to dare touching any of that code. Since #7996 does most of what I want (enable system tests), and since I don't really care about Ubuntu, I'm just going to close this. I do hope some future maintainer fixes the environment mess, and maybe that'll be me some lazy day, but not now.

> spent an hour trying to understand

A future maintainer could benefit from your research if it were encoded in the form of a jira card...even if unassigned.  Otherwise it's doubtful a future maintainer will remember to look in a closed PR for a "TODO"
